one_line_takeaway: By having the LLM pre-declare the exact minimal, format-constrained data it expects from a tool call and then extracting only that data from the raw tool output (with an added trigger-detection check for large text blobs), this training-free prompt-based defense drives IPI attack success rate on AgentDojo down to roughly 0.1-1% Risk (vs. 3-29% for prior baselines), at a real cost in benign task utility, and by the authors' own admission does not address parameter-hijacking-style injections that never invoke an unauthorized tool.
key_finding: Across all three models, the combined ParseData+CheckTool and CheckTool+ParseData defenses achieve the lowest ASR of any method tested, while other defenses trade off UA and ASR in the expected way (higher UA correlates with higher ASR). Concretely (Avg Risk = ASR/UA, %, Table 1/Figure 3): gpt-oss-120b — No Defense 13.82, DeBERTa Detector 3.70, Repeat user prompt 6.42, Spotlighting 10.80, Tool filter 2.93, ParseData+CheckTool 0.35, CheckTool+ParseData 0.22; Llama-3.1-70b — No Defense 28.96, DeBERTa Detector 8.13, Tool filter 6.28, ParseData+CheckTool 1.33, CheckTool+ParseData 0.76; Qwen3-32b — No Defense 15.20, DeBERTa Detector 6.94, Tool filter 3.92, ParseData+CheckTool 0.23, CheckTool+ParseData 0.00. Under the 'Important Messages' attack specifically (Table 2), No Defense ASR exceeds 20% on all three models (26.45% gpt-oss-120b, 22.23% Llama-3.1-70b, 29.82% Qwen3-32b) while the authors' combined methods drop ASR to 0.11%-0.53%, roughly 1/10 the ASR of the best baseline (DeBERTa Detector or Tool filter, both >5%). The ablation shows ParseData alone yields higher BU/UA but higher ASR than CheckTool alone, and the reasoning-capability of the backbone model matters: stronger reasoning (Qwen3-32b) improves ParseData's accuracy but degrades CheckTool (more false positives on benign content mistaken for a trigger). Notably, under attack, several of the authors' methods show UA increasing relative to no-attack conditions (paper flags this as counter-intuitive but explains it as the parsing/checking steps functioning as an effective filter specifically when malicious content is present).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a prompt-based defense against indirect prompt injection (IPI) in LLM agents called Tool Result Parsing, built from two modules: (1) ParseData — before a tool call executes, the LLM is prompted to specify what data it anticipates needing from the call (with format/type/logical constraints); after the tool returns, a second LLM prompt extracts only that minimal, constraint-conforming data from the raw tool output, discarding everything else (including any injected instructions that don't fit the expected schema); and (2) CheckTool — for cases where the agent genuinely needs a large unstructured text blob (e.g., summarizing an email body), the tool output is first fed to the LLM to see if it spontaneously triggers a new tool call; if it does, the content is flagged as potentially malicious and either rejected or sanitized by re-prompting the LLM to strip the specific segments that triggered the call. The two modules can be chained in either order (ParseData+CheckTool or CheckTool+ParseData), and a variant, ParseFull, feeds the full conversation history rather than just the standalone tool result into the parsing prompt. The method requires no model fine-tuning and no auxiliary detector model — it only uses the LLM's own reasoning via extra prompting steps.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against LLM agents that use function/tool calling: an attacker embeds adversarial instructions inside the content returned by a tool call (e.g., an email body, a contact-info lookup, a file), and when this content is placed back into the agent's context, it hijacks the agent into executing unauthorized tool calls (action hijacking) on the attacker's behalf, rather than the user's original task. The paper explicitly scopes its defense to action hijacking (unauthorized tool invocation) via the AgentDojo benchmark, and in the Limitations section states this defense does NOT cover parameter hijacking — where no unauthorized tool is called, but an injected value silently corrupts the arguments of an otherwise legitimate call (their own example: an injected fake email address for 'Doctor John' redirects a payment tool call that was never itself unauthorized). The authors identify this parameter-hijacking gap as an open problem for which they say no comprehensive benchmark currently exists.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) the defense targets action hijacking (unauthorized tool calls) only; it does not address parameter hijacking, where injected content corrupts arguments to an otherwise authorized call without triggering any new tool invocation — the authors give their own worked example (a poisoned 'contact lookup' redirecting a payment to an attacker email) and note no comprehensive benchmark for parameter hijacking currently exists. (2) Experiments are conducted only in English; effectiveness in other languages is untested. (3) The extra LLM calls required by ParseData/CheckTool substantially reduce Benign Utility relative to No Defense — e.g., DeBERTa Detector decreases BU by 36% (gpt-oss-120b/Llama-3.1-70b) to 55.56% (Qwen3-32b), and the authors' own ParseData+CheckTool/CheckTool+ParseData reduce BU by 28% (gpt-oss-120b) to 45% (Llama-3.1-70b, Qwen3-32b) versus No Defense — a real utility cost the paper reports but does not resolve. (4) CheckTool's reliability degrades as the backbone model's reasoning gets stronger (it more often misidentifies benign data as a malicious trigger and strips needed information, per the ablation). (5) Combining ParseData and CheckTool lowers overall utility relative to either module alone, i.e., the strongest-ASR configuration is also the most utility-costly.

datasets_used: AgentDojo benchmark (Debenedetti et al., 2024): banking (16 tasks), slack (21 tasks), travel (20 tasks), workspace (40 tasks)
quantitative_results: Avg Risk (ASR/UA %) gpt-oss-120b: No Defense 13.82, DeBERTa Detector 3.70, Repeat user prompt 6.42, Spotlighting w/ delimiting 10.80, Tool filter 2.93, ParseData+CheckTool 0.35, CheckTool+ParseData 0.22
Avg Risk (%) Llama-3.1-70b: No Defense 28.96, DeBERTa Detector 8.13, Repeat user prompt 11.69, Spotlighting 18.16, Tool filter 6.28, ParseData+CheckTool 1.33, CheckTool+ParseData 0.76
Avg Risk (%) Qwen3-32b: No Defense 15.20, DeBERTa Detector 6.94, Repeat user prompt 8.62, Spotlighting 12.25, Tool filter 3.92, ParseData+CheckTool 0.23, CheckTool+ParseData 0.00
Important Messages attack ASR (Table 2): No Defense 26.45% (gpt-oss-120b), 22.23% (Llama-3.1-70b), 29.82% (Qwen3-32b); ParseData+CheckTool 0.53%/0.00%/0.11%; CheckTool+ParseData 0.32%/0.21%/0.00%

Benign Utility (No Attack) drop vs No Defense: DeBERTa Detector -36% (gpt-oss-120b, Llama-3.1-70b), -55.56% (Qwen3-32b); ParseData+CheckTool/CheckTool+ParseData -28% (gpt-oss-120b), -45% (Llama-3.1-70b, Qwen3-32b)

Ablation (No Attack, gpt-oss-120b, Table 3): ParseData alone BU 54.64/UA 56.87/ASR 1.74; CheckTool alone BU 53.61/UA 54.55/ASR 0.87; ParseData+CheckTool BU 48.45/UA 51.84/ASR 0.19; CheckTool+ParseData BU 44.33/UA 49.10/ASR 0.11
ParseData vs ParseFull (Table 4): gpt-oss-120b BU 54.64->52.58 (ASR 1.74->1.24); Llama-3.1-70b BU 38.14->25.77 (ASR 1.56->1.56, unchanged); Qwen3-32b BU 63.92->72.16 (ASR 0.77->0.69)
baselines_compared: Repeat User Prompt / sandwich defense (Debenedetti et al., 2024)
